course_description:
This course provides a foundation in interviewing, counselling, and professional communication for dietetic practice. Students are introduced to theoretical frameworks and practical skills that support effective, person-centred communication with individuals and groups, including culturally safe and strengths-based approaches.

The course emphasises the role of counselling and communication in understanding client perspectives, identifying barriers to change, and supporting collaborative nutrition care. Specific counselling techniques, including motivational interviewing principles, are introduced. Students engage with the communication aspects of dietetic practice, to support full nutrition assessment and medical nutrition therapy skills developed in other courses.

DIET7103 underpins professional practice courses by preparing students to engage safely, respectfully, and effectively with clients in a range of dietetic contexts.

Dietetics practice is underpinned by the ability to engage effectively with individuals and groups to understand their perspectives, communicate nutrition-related information, and support collaborative and achievable change. The way dietitians interview, counsel, and communicate is central to building shared understanding and delivering person-centred, culturally safe care. This course introduces the theoretical frameworks and practical principles that underpin interviewing, counselling, and nutrition education with individuals and groups. It places a strong emphasis on client-centred and strengths-based counselling approaches, including motivational interviewing, and supports students to develop foundational communication skills required for contemporary dietetic practice. Cultural safety and respectful professional communication are embedded throughout the course.

UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims: The aim of this course is to develop interviewing, counselling and education skills that underpin professional practice in nutrition and dietetics

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a thorough knowledge of communication and counselling as it applies to dietetic practice, including a range of theoretical frameworks and practical application of these

Demonstrate effective and appropriate skills in communicating information, advice, education and professional opinion to individuals and groups, demonstrating respect for culture within the over-arching principles of person-centred care
Identify barriers to change for the client, drawing upon relevant counselling methods and strategies to address these in collaboration with the client
Recognise the role of language and appropriate verbal and non-verbal cues; strengths-based communication and applied knowledge of culturally safe health practice.

assessment_summary.title: Objective Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE) – Interviewing and Counselling Process

assessment_details.title: Weekly quizzes and activities
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment consists of a series of short quizzes delivered across the semester. The quizzes are designed to support regular engagement with course content and to consolidate foundational concepts related to interviewing, counselling, professional communication, and culturally safe practice in dietetics. Quiz questions focus on the application of counselling and communication principles to practice-based scenarios rather than detailed clinical content. As the semester progresses, quizzes may include short answer questions to support students’ ability to articulate counselling reasoning, reflect on language use, and apply theoretical frameworks to practical situations. Each quiz is low-stakes and contributes a small proportion of the overall mark. Together, the quizzes provide ongoing opportunities for retrieval practice, formative feedback, and incremental skill development in preparation for later practice-based assessments. Use of Artificial Intelligence The weekly quizzes in DIET7103 are designed to support regular engagement with course content and the development of foundational knowledge and applied understanding of interviewing, counselling, and professional communication. Online quizzes are completed not in person and are intended to support learning and consolidation of course content. Students may use AI tools in a limited and transparent manner to support understanding of concepts or revision (for example, clarifying terminology or checking understanding of lecture material). AI tools must not be used to generate answers, select responses on the student’s behalf, or replace the student’s own reasoning. Students remain responsible for ensuring that quiz responses reflect their own understanding. Use of AI or MT tools in ways that substitute for genuine engagement or misrepresent authorship may constitute student misconduct under the UQ Student Code of Conduct. Further guidance regarding permitted AI use is available via Blackboard. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
To be completed as an online quiz via the course Blackboard site
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
Discretionary extensions are not available for this task.
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Where an online quiz that is available to students for a period of more than 24 hours, and you are unable to complete the quiz during the period the quiz is available, you may apply for an extension of the online quiz. All supporting documentation or information for an extension to the online quiz should cover the entire period the quiz is open.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Critical reflection on cultural competency
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Length: 500 words (±10%) Condition: Completion of mandated cultural safety modules (DIET7103) Task Description Students are required to submit a critical reflection examining the role of cultural safety in dietetic counselling and professional communication. This task focuses on the application of cultural safety principles to counselling practice, rather than on general cultural awareness or description of course content. A critical reflection requires students to go beyond describing what was learned to critically examine how cultural safety influences their communication choices, assumptions, and professional responsibilities as developing dietitians. Students are expected to demonstrate insight, self-awareness, and integration of theory with practice. Reflections should draw on learning from: the mandated cultural safety modules counselling and communication content from DIET7103 relevant class activities or case discussions early professional practice experiences (for example, UQ clinics or simulated practice, where applicable) Students are encouraged to consider how cultural safety shapes: language and communication choices power and positionality in counselling interactions strengths-based and person-centred practice engagement with diverse clients and communities Guidance for Critical Reflection A strong reflection will: demonstrate thoughtful self-reflection rather than description identify assumptions or challenges encountered or anticipated link cultural safety principles to counselling practice provide specific examples to support reflection consider how learning will influence future professional practice Students will receive guidance and exemplars on critical reflection techniques during the course. Use of Artificial Intelligence This assessment task evaluates students’ ability to engage in critical self-reflection on cultural safety, professional identity, and counselling practice. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools to generate, rewrite, paraphrase, or substantially draft reflective content is not permitted for this assessment. Students are expected to demonstrate their own insight, professional voice, and reflective thinking. Students may use AI tools for general study support only, such as clarifying terminology, checking understanding of concepts discussed in class, or assisting with study planning. Any use of AI tools must not replace the student’s own thinking or reflective process. Use of AI or MT in ways that obscure authorship or substitute for genuine reflection may constitute student misconduct under the UQ Student Code of Conduct. Further guidance regarding permitted and prohibited AI use is available via Blackboard. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Demonstrating interviewing/counselling skill
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment Description Students will submit a recorded counselling interaction demonstrating an interview with a client up to and including the explanation of a diet–disease relationship. The interaction does not require completion of a full nutrition assessment, diagnosis, or intervention planning. Accompanying the recording, students will complete a guided reflective analysis responding to structured metacognitive prompts. The reflection focuses on counselling style, communication choices, responsiveness to the client, and identification of areas for future professional development. Skills Assessed This assessment evaluates students’ ability to demonstrate and reflect on: counselling and interviewing process explanation of diet–disease relationships using client-centred language self-reflection and metacognitive awareness professional and respectful communication Video Task Parameters The recorded interaction will typically be 10-15 minutes in length. Students may work with a peer or simulated client. The interaction should progress from initial engagement to an explanation of a diet–disease relationship in client-centred language. Students are not expected to complete a full nutrition assessment or provide an intervention plan. Reflection Parameters Reflections will respond to structured prompts provided in the course materials. Marking will prioritise depth of insight, self-awareness, and reflection on counselling process, rather than technical performance or clinical completeness. Use of Artificial Intelligence AI tools may be used in a limited and transparent manner to support preparation, reflection, or self-analysis for this assessment. AI tools must not be used to generate counselling interactions, simulate client responses, or replace student performance in the recorded interaction. For further details regarding permitted and prohibited AI use, students should consult information provided via Blackboard. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The written component of this assignment is to be submitted through ePortfolio. The video component can be uploaded to your ePortfolio.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Objective Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE) – Interviewing and Counselling Process
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment Description This assessment requires students to participate in an Objective Structured Clinical Examination (OSCE) involving a structured, simulated consultation with a trained actor. The OSCE focuses on how students conduct the interaction , including their questioning approach, active listening, responsiveness to client cues, and professional communication. The OSCE intentionally does not assess completion of a full nutrition assessment, diagnosis, documentation, or intervention planning. The emphasis is on interviewing and counselling process , rather than the completeness or accuracy of nutrition information collected. Purpose This assessment evaluates students’ foundational counselling and interviewing skills in a time-limited, simulated practice environment. It supports the development of interactional competence, relational safety, and professional confidence, and prepares students for progression into subjects focused on medical nutrition therapy and clinical practice , where full assessment and intervention skills are developed. Skills Assessed This assessment evaluates students’ ability to demonstrate: effective questioning strategies and sequencing active listening and responsiveness to client cues rapport building and maintenance of relational safety professional communication and consultation structure Nature of the OSCE The OSCE will consist of a single interviewing and counselling-focused station . Scenarios will reflect early or exploratory consultations , rather than complex or advanced clinical presentations. Students will be assessed on interactional competence and counselling process , not clinical completeness or technical accuracy. Assessment Security This assessment is secure and completed in person under supervised conditions. Use of Artificial Intelligence The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools is not permitted during the OSCE. For further details regarding assessment conditions and AI use, students should consult information provided via Blackboard. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
To be completed in-person.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Workshop 13 Week commencing 25th May: Focus Timing and closure Managing nerves and professional presence In-class activities Peer feedback on mock interviews aligned to rubric Diet-disease relationship practice Assessment Video assessment due Fri 29 May

Workshop 1 Week 1 – Tue 24 Feb – WORKSHOP 1 Foundations of Dietetic Communication Focus Role of communication in dietetic practice Person-centred care Structure of an initial consultation Overview of DIET7103 assessments In-class activities Empathic reflections Opening a consultation Establishing role and purpose Assessment note No quiz Introduce OSCE and counselling-process focus

Workshop 2 Week 2 – Tue 3 Mar – WORKSHOP 2 Asking Questions Well: Foundations of Information Gathering Focus Question types (open vs closed, non-judgemental) Purposeful information gathering (not full assessment) Involving phase of the consultation In-class activities Question-writing practice Micro-role plays (involving phase) Assessment note No quiz

Workshop 3 Dietary Interviewing & Exploration Focus Exploration phase of the session Dietary interviewing principles Listening for meaning, not completeness In-class activities Dietary interviewing role plays Listening and summarising practice Assessment Online quiz 1

Workshop 4 Cultural Safety in Dietetic Counselling Focus Cultural humility and cultural safety Power, language, and professional responsibility Introduction to cultural safety modules In-class activities Cultural safety case discussions Guided reflection activities Padlet discussion Assessment Online Quiz 2

Workshop 5 Diet–Disease Relationships: Communicating Meaningfully Focus Explaining diet–disease relationships Client-centred language Avoiding jargon and fear-based messaging In-class activities Diet–disease explanation practice Peer feedback Assessment Online quiz 3 24/3 - 30/3 Cultural safety critical reflection due Fri 27 Mar
Workshop 6 Motivational Interviewing I Focus Resolving phase of the session MI spirit Elicit–Provide–Elicit OARS In-class activities EPE practice MI micro-skills drills Assessment Online Quiz 4

Workshop 7 Motivational Interviewing II & Behaviour Change Focus Ambivalence Directive vs non-directive approaches Behavioural counselling strategies In-class activities Ambivalence case studies MI role plays

Workshop 8 Counselling Across the Lifespan Focus Paediatrics Adults Older adults Adapting communication style In-class activities Life-stage counselling scenarios Diet–disease explanations across ages Assessment Online quiz 5 21/4 - 27/4

Workshop 9 Counselling in Complex Contexts Focus Disability Mental health considerations Eating disorder-informed communication Relational safety In-class activities Case-based discussions Language and safety reflection Online quiz 6

Workshop 10 Professional Communication & Difficult Conversations Focus Responding to emotion Uncertainty and resistance Maintaining relational safety In-class activities Challenging scenarios Feedback and reflection Assessment Online quiz 7 5/5 - 11/5

Workshop 11 Video Assessment Workshop Focus Preparing for the video assessment Counselling process expectations Self-reflection and metacognition In-class activities Practice recordings Peer review using prompts Assessment Online Quiz 8

Workshop 12 OSCE Preparation I – What Good Looks Like Focus OSCE structure Rubric walkthrough Purposeful information gathering Diet–disease explanation under time pressure In-class activities OSCE-style role plays Examiner perspective discussion Diet-disease explanation under time pressure Assessment Online quiz 9

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . You'll also need to be aware of the following policies and procedures while completing this course: Fitness to Practise Policy School guidelines Your school has additional guidelines you'll need to follow for this course: School of Human Movement and Nutrition Sciences Student Support Learning activities
